title: Widdringtonia nodiflora
intro: Widdringtonia nodiflora, also known as the White Stinkwood or False Yellowwood, is a medicinal plant species native to southern Africa. Traditionally, the plant has been used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antispasmodic properties, which contribute to its potential health benefits. The bioactive constituents of Widdringtonia nodiflora include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Widdringtonia nodiflora is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is used to treat a range of health conditions, including colds, coughs, and respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Widdringtonia nodiflora is classified within the family Podocarpaceae, a group of coniferous plants commonly known as the yellowwoods. This family is part of the order Pinales, which comprises coniferous trees and shrubs. The order Pinales is further grouped under the class Pinopsida, a division of coniferous plants. Pinopsida is a subclass of the division Gymnospermae, which comprises seed-producing plants that do not have flowers. Widdringtonia nodiflora's classification is further detailed at the species level, but its broad categorization is within these groups.

The plant's bark, leaves, and roots are said to possess antibacterial and antifungal properties.
